name: Zirconium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Zirconium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Zirconium oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Zirconium's moderate absorption and thermal processing

spotSize.description: Optimal beam diameter for balancing cleaning efficiency and processing speed

repetitionRate.description: High repetition rate for efficient surface coverage while maintaining thermal control

energyDensity.description: Fluence optimized for Zirconium oxide ablation threshold while preserving base material

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide layer ablation with minimal heat affected zone

scanSpeed.description: Scanning speed providing effective oxide removal with controlled thermal input

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for thorough oxide removal with minimal substrate impact

overlapRatio.description: Beam overlap percentage ensuring complete surface coverage without excessive thermal buildup

dwellTime.description: Effective interaction time per spot for controlled material removal

power.description: Typical range for pulsed fiber lasers cleaning reflective metals like zirconium; starting at 150 W balances efficiency and avoids overheating due to Zr's high reflectivity (~90% at 1064 nm). Max safe before damage ~500 W based on thermal conductivity (21 W/mK). Source: IPG Photonics specs and studies on metal decontamination (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective for light contamination (e.g., oxides) at higher speeds; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy buildup. Recommended 1500 mm/s optimizes cleaning rate while minimizing heat input on Zr's melting point (1855°C). Source: Industry manuals (e.g., CleanLaser systems) and process optimization in Nucl. Eng. Des. 2020.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for industrial cleaning of Zr to ablate contaminants with minimal HAZ; picosecond (5-50 ps) for ultra-low thermal effects but costlier. 20 ns recommended to limit plasma shielding on reflective surfaces. Source: Laser physics reviews (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2019) and ablation studies on Zr alloys.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for 1064 nm cleaning; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation on low-conductivity Zr. 30 kHz balances throughput and safety. Trade-off: faster cleaning vs. potential subsurface heating. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2021).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal (e.g., oxides, rust) without ablating Zr substrate; damage threshold ~2.5-4 J/cm² due to high reflectivity. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe surface prep. Conservative for Zr's properties. Source: Ablation threshold studies on Zr (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017; similar to Ti alloys).

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (100-200 μm) for fine detail on Zr components (e.g., nuclear cladding); larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform energy distribution on reflective surfaces. Source: Equipment specs (e.g., Coherent lasers) and industrial case studies.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination; multi-pass (2-3) for heavy layers to avoid damage. Recommended 2 passes for complete Zr oxide removal without excessive heat. Source: Industry practice in nuclear decontamination (e.g., IAEA reports) and case studies on Zr alloys.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage on irregular Zr surfaces; 50% for speed, 80% for thoroughness. 70% recommended to prevent gaps while minimizing over-processing and heat buildup. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Laser Technol. 2022) for metal cleaning.
